turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court this morning in case 17-647, Knick v. Township of Scott. Rose Mary Knick owns 90 acres of land in Scott Township, Pennsylvania. Knick's property includes a single-family residence where she resides, a grazing area for horses, and as is as relevant today, a small graveyard where some ancestors of Knick's neighbors are allegedly buried. Such family cemeteries are fairly common in rural Pennsylvania, where backyard burials have been permitted for a long time. Now, the Township passed an ordinance in 2012 requiring that all cemeteries, including small graveyard on private property like Knick's, be kept open and accessible to the general public during daylight hours. Now, Knick filed a lawsuit in Federal Court alleging that by opening up her land to all comers, the ordinance took her property without just compensation in violation of the Takings Clause of the Fifth Amendment. Normally a plaintiff complaining of unconstitutional conduct by state or local officials is able to proceed directly to Federal Court under the Civil Rights Act of 1871. But 34 years ago, in a case called Williamson County v. Hamilton Bank, we held that a takings plaintiff like Knick cannot bring her federal claim in Federal Court until she has sought and been denied compensation in State Court under state law. So the District Court dismissed Knick's complaint. The Third Circuit Court of Appeals affirmed and we granted review to reconsider our decision in Williamson County. The Williamson County Court held that a property owner has no federal takings claim until he is litigated and lost in State Court, because it believed that a government does not necessarily violate the Takings Clause at the time it takes property without paying for it. All the clause requires at the time of the taking Williamson County thought is that the government give the property owner some means of obtaining compensation after the fact. A violation occurs, if at all, only later when the government denies the property owner compensation in such a post-taking proceeding. Since there is no violation at the time of the taking itself, the property owner cannot go to Federal Court at that time, because you can only go to Federal Court to remedy a violation. Now, the Williamson County Court anticipated that an unsuccessful takings plaintiff would be able to go to Federal Court with the federal takings claim when the State Court denies him compensation. There would be a violation at that time. But 20 years later, we issued another decision called San Remo Hotel v. San Francisco. There we said that once a State Court denies a takings claim, the plaintiff does not get to start over fresh in Federal Court. He is pretty much stuck with whatever the State Court decided. So you can see that the takings plaintiff is in a catch 22. He cannot go to Federal Court without going to State Court first, that's what Williamson County says, but if he goes to State Court and loses, his claim will be barred in Federal Court. That's what San Remo says. Now that should tip us off that something is wrong, and we decide today that the something that's wrong is Williamson County. Contrary to Williamson County, a property owner has a claim for a violation of the Takings Clause as soon as the government takes his property without paying for it. The Takings Clause says, nor shall private property be taken for public use without just compensation. It does not say, nor shall private property be taken for public use without an available procedure that will result in just compensation down the road. Now, if a local government takes private property without paying for it, that government has violated the Fifth Amendment just as the Takings Clause says without regard to subsequent State Court proceedings. Now, that does not mean that the government has to pay compensation right away. It can pay you later, so long as it pays interest back to the time of the taking. But the important thing is that as soon as it takes the property without paying for it, it has violated the Takings Clause at that time. Now, why is that important? Because if the government violated the Takings Clause at that time, that's when you get to go into Federal Court. You don't have to wait for any subsequent state proceeding and that means you don't have a San Remo problem. So Williamson County was wrong. The next question is whether we should overrule it or whether stare decisis, Latin for let the decision stand, counsels in favor of sticking with it despite its flaws. Earlier this week we turned aside two requests to overrule prior decisions, but today the balance tips in favor of dispensing with Williamson County. First, Williamson County was not just wrong, its reasoning conflicted with much of our established Takings Clause jurisprudence, and the decision has come in for repeated criticism over the years from justices of this Court and respected commentators. Indeed, no party here even defends the decision's original reasoning. Because of its shaky foundations, the state litigation requirement has been a rule in search of a justification for over 30 years. We quickly abandoned Williamson County's anomalous conclusion that a property owner does not acquire a Fifth Amendment right at the time of the taking and have since described the state litigation requirement as instead a prudential ripeness rule. No party defends that approach either. The state litigation requirement has proved to be an unworkable rule in practice. As explained, Williamson County envisioned that takings plaintiffs would be able to bring their claims in Federal Court after losing in State Court. But the Court failed to consider that, as we later held in San Remo, that the State Court judgment would effectively bar any federal suit. The Williamson County Court did not realize that its holding would keep Takings Claims against local governments out of the Federal Courts. There was nothing in the briefs or arguments about that and nothing in the opinion. Finally, there are no reliance interests on Williamson County. We have recognized that the force of stare decisis is reduced when it comes to rules that do not provide a guide to lawful behavior. Our holding that uncompensated takings violate the Fifth Amendment at the time of the taking will not force governments to do anything differently. The same rules apply about what does and does not constitute a taking. Our holding simply means that Takings Claims against local governments will be able to be brought in Federal Court without plaintiffs having to go to State Court first, just like claims under any other provision of the Bill of Rights. Now, for those reasons, the state litigation requirement of Williamson County is overruled. Ms. Knick need not ask a Pennsylvania Court to order the Township to compensate her for turning part of her property into a public cemetery. A Federal Court, no less than a State Court, is competent to adjudicate her constitutional claim. The judgment of the United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Kagan has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justices Ginsburg, Breyer and Sotomayor join.
